speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Mrs. Mehr in Melbourne-- sunny Melbourne-- very, very sunny-- sunny Melbourne, Australia, on the 20th of February, 1997. Interviewer, Richard [INAUDIBLE]. Good morning, Mrs. Mehr.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ida Mehr.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the surname is M--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mehr.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-E-H-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac Spiegler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-P-I-E-G-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her married name, or her previous surname?

speaker: interviewer
text: First name and maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shewa. Maiden name was Korb. K-O-R-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had five siblings, excluding me. I was the sixth. Two brothers, and three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: The names of the two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph and Wolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the three sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah. One survivor, Ella. Previous name, her married name is Christina De Carli. She lives in France. Her husband was-- she married during the war, and her husband was non-Jewish. He passed away a few years ago. She lives in Marseille now. And she visited me-- five years ago she was here. And I found out about her-- that she is alive at all-- 20 years after the war. I searched for her, and she searched for me. I went to United States from here, from Australia, to the HIAS, because I've heard that they have names of people-- survivors of the Holocaust. So I went to United States. And, indeed, I found out from them that she sent all the particulars to them, to search for me, because she couldn't find me. And there I found out that she's married now under the name De Carli and lives in France.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they cabled for me straight away. And yes, of course, she was my sister. And so I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they booked for me a passage-- ship-- to France. And I went straight from there to France. I let to know my daughter, first, here, that I found a sister alive and that I'm going to France. I stayed a half a year with her and her husband. He was alive then, still. And together we remembered, of course, our parents. She didn't know. She had no idea who survived or who not. Because she lived in France, still, 20 years after the war, as a Christian. Her husband didn't like to review her story. So he kept it a secretions there. But when I came over there, I said, no, my sister's Jewish, I am Jewish, and I want everybody to know that she is not a Christian anymore. The war is over, she has nothing to fear now, and to tell the truth to everybody. So she did. And, since then, a few years later, her husband had a heart attack-- passed away. She moved. It was a small town where she lived-- Berre-l'Etang. She moved to Marseille to be nearer Jewish people and went to a synagogue there. Now she lives as normal, as a Jewish person with Jewish friends, going to synagogue for holidays. And we are in contact. That's the story how I found my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had three sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three sisters-- I was the fourth-- and two brothers. Together, we were six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, again, the names of your three sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the eldest sister was Sarah. Later was the brother Joseph-- one after the other. Then was Rachel, then it was myself, Ella, the one in France, and Wolf, who was killed by the Nazis. They all were killed, except Ella and myself, of course. So we had two survivors. And later I'll tell the story about my child, who was born in '39. And, after the whole story, I have to tell it, how she survived. Now she lives here. She's married. She's not anymore Mehr. She's Wald-- W-A-L-D-- 'walt,' Wald-- and lives with her husband and three children. I have three grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- excuse me-- which town were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was born? Grodek, by Lwow. It's about 25 kilometers from Lwow. It's actually like a big suburb, but it's a city for itself. Grodek Jagiellonski. And, as I said, my name was Spiegler.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would have lived in that town, when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many people, you want to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not exactly, just roughly,

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about, uh-- it was a small township and was a very active-- very active Jewry-- mostly Zionists. There were a few Hasids. But mostly Zionistic orientated. And there would have been about 80,000, approximately. I don't know exactly. There was only one synagogue. The old times, that was called like a 'shtiebel.' And my father used to go there, although he was not a Hasid, every Shabbat-- not during the week-- and my mother only on High Holy Days. Otherwise, not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of the population of about 80,000 which is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of the population of the city, in Grodek, would you say it was majority of Jewish population, or minority?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Grodek? Minority, rather.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They mostly lived in a part. It wasn't really a ghetto. But it was like a ghetto. They all concentrated in one part of Grodek. We didn't live there. We lived rather in a part with more Gentiles. And Poles and Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you describe the composition of the town? Was it roughly a third Jewish people, a third Ukrainians, a third Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Poles, less Ukrainians-- not many. But there were about, I wouldn't say one third-- less than a third.

speaker: interviewer
text: A fifth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was quite a-- a-- a big Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, about 10% Ukrainians-- 10% or 20% Ukrainians--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Ukrainians, about 30% Jewish, and about 60% Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: The largest was the Polish community, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned, just before, that you lived in the Gentile part of the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any particular activities which were exclusive to the Jewish population? In other words, were some types of shops mainly the Jewish types of shops? In other words, were the delicatessens or the food shops--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were in small shops. And-- otherwise not-- not especially Jewish, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Jewish people do for a living, in the city that you grew up in-- in Grodek? What did they mostly do for a living? The Jewish people-- what did they do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shopkeepers-- small shopkeepers-- and merchants. Some had family business-- like my husband's family. He came from Grodek, too. They had family business. Parts of machinery-- I don't remember exactly, because I was very young then. But they were quite-- then considered wealthy people. And my husband, otherwise-- a-- they had a-- his grandfather had a-- a mill-- you know, producing flour-- a mill there. I remember still the place where it was, the mill. It was in the city-- not outside, but in a-- a bit further-- a street out of the city. A very big mill. I don't know how you call it. And they produced flour from--

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Flour mill.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was my husband's. And they lived there. There were two buildings accommodating the families. They were quite a big family. And they were considered quite wealthy people, then. It's different now, of course. You can't compare.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you remember what the Ukrainians mostly did for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I know they were great anti-Semites-- the Ukraines more than the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you think that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, it often happens the children were afraid of the Ukraines. Because they had brothers and sisters, and there were a few men beaten up by then. Especially when it came-- uh-- [POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Christmas? Yes? Jews were afraid to go out of their houses, even. Especially in the evenings. We were afraid. I remember, as a child, my mother said, never go out in the evening. Because around, we had neighbors-- Ukrainians. And I remember-- I'll tell later the story about the Ukrainians. They helped the Germans to round up the Jews. They knew exactly where they live, where they are, to round them up. And they were eventually all killed, among them my parents and my brother-- youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: We knew that they are great anti-Semites. Worse than the Poles. So were the Ukrainians also shopkeepers, or were they tradespeople?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I don't know what they did exactly. But I know what they did to us Jews. But what they do for a living, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe it was manual jobs-- more laboring jobs, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No one would-- Jews wouldn't employ them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: And others-- the Poles, I don't know. I don't know what the Poles did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were a lot of Polish shops, as well, in Grodek.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were. There were a lot of Jewish, but not so many Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what types of--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were. But the schools were Polish. The high school which I intended were Polish. There were a few Polish doctors. There were Jews, too. There were three Jewish doctors. And the chemists' shops, there were two Jewish, not others. [BACKGROUND NOISE] That's all I can remember, as a child. Because, later on, after finishing schooling, I went to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the butcher shops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Butcher shops? No-- they were mostly non-Jewish. But there were kosher ones. They kept it in their homes. They didn't openly have Jewish butcher's shops. I don't remember any. I remember my mother used to go and buy meat to-- it was called the 'Jewish shtetl.' And 'shtetl' was like-- like a ghetto. Nobody called it a ghetto, but it was-- there lived Jews only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another part of Grodek.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Grodek. I remember exactly where it was. But it was another part. Jews lived scattered among non-Jews, too. But mostly they were concentrated there. And there they had in their homes kept kosher meat. And I think they bought them through the shokhtem, who did ritual--

speaker: interviewer
text: Slaughter. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --killing-- slaughter. So they kept it in their home. They bought it from them, somehow. I don't know exactly the whole procedure. But I remember my mother used to go there. Even I remember the name of the family we used to sell kosher meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Michel. But others I don't know. I wasn't interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the [NON-ENGLISH], the food shops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other shops were small ones. First of all, there was a small marketplace. And there where the non-Jewish butchers were situated. And other shops. That was a small marketplace, one. And it was another marketplace, every, to say, twice weekly, in the center of the city. There, people used to go and buy whatever they needed-- fruit and other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: In stalls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and there were-- no, there were little shops where we lived-- small shops, I remember, around the corner where I lived with my family-- my parents. Around the corner, there were a few Jewish small shops.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selling delicatessen products?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish-- Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the schools. What sort of schools were there in Grodek? There were public schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a public school. And, further down, on Lwowska-- Ulitsa Lwowska-- that was a road [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] leading to Lwow-- that's why it was called Lwowska-- there was one only high school, but it didn't have a special name. It was in Grodek, a high school. Everybody knew the high school is there. So, one public and one high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gimnazjum.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any ethnic schools for Jews or Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No special. They were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a cheder attached to the synagogue-- a Jewish cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a synagogue, and nearby was a cheder, yes. And Jewish children attended the cheder and the synagogue. But there were not different synagogues for Hasidim and for others. They were all together there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend the cheder, or just the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself? A cheder? A cheder was only for boys-- for little boys from three years on. And public school-- everybody had to go to a public school, because the high school, the gimnazjum, was public, too. There was no private schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was their separate Jewish school for girls-- Jewish girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-- yes. Yes. I remember at our school-- not in the public-- gimnazjum. It was the same school-- part for girls, part for boys. Not together. In our-- when I attended gimnazjum, there were no boys, only girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were going to the first school-- the-- first--

speaker: interviewee
text: The primary?  Public? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --first primary school, what was your life like, there? Did you have a very easy time with the teachers and students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very easy. I loved it. But there were some teachers, very anti-Semitic. Some of them. But I was always lucky, because I was considered a very good student-- a bright one. And I had friends among non-Jews, and the teachers loved me, too. As a matter of fact, we had to write a story-- I remember exactly-- from history, from Stefan Batory. And I wrote the whole story of his life. I had to research it and to stand up. And I was very young, then. And stood up and read what I wrote. And the teacher said, you can see [POLISH]. How she writes Polish, how her grammar is, you have to be ashamed. She was not anti-Semitic. She liked me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she said, would you like your mother to come to see us? Yes. And my mother came. And they said, who speaks, at home, such a good Polish? My mother spoke a very good Polish. They said, you're exceptional, because Jews don't speak well Polish-- not 'gramatyczny.' Was that true of that period?  Was that true of that time?

words.text: period?  Was that true of that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because I remember that my mother used to say that she was ashamed some mothers-- some Jewish people-- did not attend school. And they didn't know how to write Polish or read Polish, and she was ashamed of it. And she said, everybody, when you live in a country, as we do, you should learn the language of the country. My mother was a very intelligent [BACKGROUND NOISE] and educated person, as I said later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's philosophy about that-- was that a common philosophy amongst Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because the Poles were-- as I said before, the Ukraines were very anti-Semitic. And, they too, they-- they didn't like the Jews. The Poles didn't like the Jews. That was the reason. And the Jews didn't like the Poles. There were not many good neighbors, as we have here-- Polish ones. But some were. Like, we lived, as I mentioned before, on a part of Grodek where there were just scattered a few Jewish families. We lived near a Ukrainian church. And my mother used-- around the corner, somehow she met the priest of the church. In Polish we say [POLISH]. She met him around the corner, somehow. And he liked very much my mother, because he said there are not many people with whom he can talk. He talked with my mother. She loved to talk about religions-- not only Jewish, other religions. And they formed just a friendship. And when the Germans came, he offered my mother to hide them in his church-- my mother, father, and youngest brother. All others were in Lwow, like my eldest sister, my brother, myself, younger sister-- two sisters. But the youngest, Wolf, was with my parents, there, still. It was years later. And he hid my mother in his church, in a cellar-- and my father and the youngest brother, Wolf-- during the German occupation. He fed them and kept him for quite-- I don't know exactly, because I haven't been there, but quite a number of months, since the beginning of the German occupation. At this time, the Germans didn't find out the Jews. They didn't look for them. That was at the beginning. No one knew about the killings or other atrocities. But later-- the whole story, I found out from someone after the war. I didn't know about it, because I was in Lwow. Finally, they had a young-- they didn't have any chil-- that priest didn't have any children. The family consisted only of himself and a wife. But there was a boy employed by him, to look after the-- he had some cows. So he looked after the cows. That boy noticed once that the priest's wife carried downstairs some food. He have seen it. And most Gentiles said, if they find out about Jews and tell the Gestapo, you will be paid for it. He ran quickly to the Gestapo and told them that he's suspecting, at the church, some hidden Jews. Then they came and got out my parents. They would probably have survived if not of that boy. But they didn't send then Jews. That was at the beginning. A few months later. They didn't send him to the camps, as it came out later. But especially boys-- Jewish boys. It was such a hatred. They took the boy, my youngest brother. He was then bar mitvahed and 13 years old. They took him to the center of the city. All the Jews rounded up-- all had to be around. They took my brother into the center. My mother, they made her look. She couldn't avert her face from it. And they beat him so long that he was a mess. First he cried-- my mother, later, told me. Before Lwow had the ghetto, I think I told you she had lot of courage-- she came to Lwow. Not as a Jewess but as a peasant. She told me this story. And he-- she had to look. They made-- if she turned away her face, they made her look. And they hit him so long until he was a mess-- fell a mess of blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll finish the first tape here and continue the second tape.
